text: Come here.
Hey guys and happy new year. I wish you
all the best this year. Let's start it
off with another George Lucas answers
video. And I figured we would start the
year off with George explaining the no
scene by Darth Vader in Revenge of the
Sith. Now, George always said that
Revenge of the Sith isn't the story of a
man turning [music] evil. It's the story
of a man losing his soul. The moment
Anakin Skywalker became Darth Vader
isn't when he kills Mace or when he
kneels before Cidius. It's when the mask
seals, when the mechanical [music]
breathing begins, that sound, the
rhythmic gasp, and if you pause frame
during the film, when he's getting his
mask on, you can actually see him mouth
the words, "Help me, Padme." That scene
is really the voice of a man who can't
die, but also can't live. Now, in the
making [music] of Revenge of the Sith,
George said this, "He's alive, but he's
lost everything." And that was really
George's vision for the creation of
Vader. It wasn't a birth, but more so a
punishment, a resurrection without
purpose. He tells the crew this. This
isn't a victory scene, it's a tragedy.
And George describes it as a
Frankenstein moment, but not in the way
that most people think. It's not so much
about horror, it's about guilt. Vader's
reconstruction is the Force's cosmic
irony. that Anakin spends an entire
trilogy trying to stop death and ends up
in tmbed in it. He wanted to save life
and now he's trapped in a machine that
only simulates it. It's the evil of
trying to control death. That's the core
of the [music] story, says George. When
Palpatine watches the droids rebuild
him, he's not looking at an apprentice.
He's looking at a [music] monument to
the failure of compassion. The man who
couldn't let go of his fear has finally
become its prisoner. Every part of
Vader's design carries meaning. The
armor isn't a symbol of strength. It's
an echo of control. The chest plate, the
respirator, the mechanical limbs, all
these things that Anakin thought would
make him invincible are now what make
him fragile. He has power, but without
freedom. And George told Hayden
Christensen during the shooting of this
scene this exactly. He says, "You're not
reborn here. You're trapped." And that's
why the operating room isn't framed like
a ascension. It's framed like a
crucifixion. George even staged the
moment as a reverse birth. Smoke instead
of breath, screams instead of cries, and
machinery instead of midwives. It's the
visual opposite of the twin births
happening across the galaxy. Padme gives
life, and Vader receives imitation.
George says, "Padme's death and Vader's
birth are the same event. One dies as
the other is reborn. Now, Ben Bert, the
sound designer, said George gave him one
specific note about that breath. Make it
sound human but wrong. And that's really
the whole idea is that the dark side
doesn't create, it corrupts. Even life
itself becoming synthetic. Now, the
armor is not just a prison for Anakin's
body. It's a metaphor for his
philosophy. The more you try to control
life, the less alive you become. Now,
when Vader asks, "Where is Padme? Is she
safe? Is she all right? He's not a
villain. He's a child. And George said
this to Hayden. He said he's not angry
yet. He's scared. And that's kind of
what makes the next line, "You killed
her so devastating. Because it's the
final lie that shocks him inside
forever." Palpatine wins not because
he's powerful, but because he convinces
Anakin that redemption is quite
impossible. Now, Matthew Stove, who
wrote the Revenge of the Sith
novelization, describes the moment like
a funeral for the soul. He can hear
Padme's voice calling his name through
the Force. And he cannot answer. The
suit cannot [music] weep. And so, that's
why George called the scream the moment
the man inside finally dies. The sound
isn't over the top. It's pain turned
mechanical. A soul trying to leave the
body, but trapped by the very power that
was supposed to save it. He's becoming
the very thing he feared, says George.
Someone who can't die, but also can't
live. Now, George told his editors to
intercut Padme's funeral with Vader's
awakening because he wanted the audience
to understand that one life's end is
another's imprisonment. Padme's death is
natural, he says. Vader's life isn't.
And that's the tragedy. The soft light
of Nibou, real air, real earth, real
beauty, contrasts with the sterile
horror of the operating chamber. Two
worlds, two philosophies. One accepts
the flow of the force. The other resists
it. Now, it's also about the entire
prequel era, I think. You know, the
Jedi, the Republic, the Sith, all of
them tried to control what can't be
controlled, and all of them end up
mechanical. The Republic becomes an
empire. The Jedi become ghosts. And
Anakin becomes a machine. But even as
darkness falls, George plants the seed
of balance. Padme's last words. There is
still good in him. Compassion can't die
completely, even inside the armor. And
that's what makes Vader's story human
instead of hopeless. Star Wars is about
love and forgiveness, says George. And
he meant that literally. You know, the
Force can't destroy evil because the
Force isn't vengeance, it's renewal. And
even though the galaxy doesn't know it
yet, the compassion that Padme believed
in, the one thing that could never be
mechanized, still lives in Anakin,
buried beneath metal and regret. Now,
years later, when Luke removes that
mask, the story finally closes the loop.
The first breath of Darth Vader becomes
his last. The machine stops fighting and
the man returns. So, George told Hayden
this. He says, "The mask is his prison.
Taking it off is his salvation." And the
sound design really mirrors it
perfectly. That same mechanical breath,
the sound that once symbolized
damnation, fades into silence. And the
first breath and the last breath are the
same note reversed. And that's really
quite the poetry of Star Wars. The
galaxy begins with a boy who can't let
go of fear, and it ends with a man who
finally does. George said it best when
he said Vader's story is about the
redemption of a man who was so afraid to
die that he destroyed himself. And
that's kind of the whole moral of Star
Wars is that the more you try to control
life, the less of it you have. The
moment you surrender, you become one
with the force again. And so I think,
you know, the birth of Darth Vader isn't
the rise of a villain. It's the death of
everything human. And the breath that
follows isn't power. It's him being
intombed. It's imprisonment. What do you
guys think of that no scene in Revenge
of the Sith? In some concept arts, Vader
was actually supposed to crush
everything in the room and have a
massive whirlwind, a storm force storm
whirlwind, and it was going to be pretty
epic. Things were supposed to go on fire
and explode, but uh it didn't happen. We
just got the crushing of a couple tin
cans essentially. Let me know what you
guys think about it. I hope you enjoyed
this kind of video. If you did, leave a
like, show some support, and I'll get
right to work on making more. If there's
a topic you have in mind that you want
answers to, you want to know what George
thinks about certain scenes or
characters in Star Wars, write them down
in the comments, and I will try to find
that for you. Have a great rest of your
day, and may the force be with you
always. [music]
